blocks.text: Hear this word that the Lord has spoken against you, O people of Israel, against the whole family that I brought up out of the land of Egypt:
2 : , knowledge here refers to a relationship through covenant. The phrase recalls traditions about divine promises to Abram (Gen 12.3) and Moses (Ex 19.4–6; Deut 7.6).
You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.
3.1–2: A chosen people are held to a higher standard.
Do two walk together unless they have made an appointment?
Does a lion roar in the forest, when it has no prey? Does a young lion cry out from its den, if it has caught nothing?
Does a bird fall into a snare on the earth, when there is no trap for it? Does a snare spring up from the ground, when it has taken nothing?
Is a trumpet blown in a city, and the people are not afraid? Does disaster befall a city, unless the Lord has done it?
6 : , the shofar, the ram's horn.
Surely the Lord God does nothing, without revealing his secret to his servants the prophets.

7 : , for positive and negative illustrations of this principle, see, respectively, Gen 18.17–19 and 2 Kings 4.27. Because the phrase “his servants the prophets” is Deuteronomistic (see 2 Kings 21.10), and because the verse is in a different form, some scholars consider it a later addition (see 2.3–4n.).
The lion has roared; who will not fear? The Lord God has spoken; who can but prophesy?

3.3–8: A series of rhetorical questions, which permit only a single response, build to an inescapable conclusion in v. 8b: The call to prophecy, unsolicited, cannot be resisted (7.14–15). Furthermore, the self-evident truths of vv. 1–8a render the audience susceptible to a truth in 8b it would naturally resist: Amos speaks on behalf of the .

8 : Just as in chs 1–2, seven indictments against other peoples (1.3–2.5) rhetorically set a trap, sprung in the eighth against Israel itself, in this chapter seven questions (3.3–6) build to a crescendo; cf. 7.14–15 for Amos's reluctance to accept the call to prophecy.

Proclaim to the strongholds in Ashdod, and to the strongholds in the land of Egypt, and say, “Assemble yourselves on Mount Samaria, and see what great tumults are within it, and what oppressions are in its midst.”
9 : , a Philistine city on the Mediterranean coast; see 1.6–8. , capital of the Northern Kingdom.
They do not know how to do right, says the Lord, those who store up violence and robbery in their strongholds.
Therefore thus says the Lord God: An adversary shall surround the land, and strip you of your defense; and your strongholds shall be plundered.
11 : God addresses Samaria directly and promises the coming of an , though whether the figure is a divine or human agent of God's judgment is difficult to say (cf. Isa 10.5; Jer 25.8; Lam 2.5).

Thus says the Lord: As the shepherd rescues from the mouth of the lion two legs, or a piece of an ear, so shall the people of Israel who live in Samaria be rescued, with the corner of a couch and part of a bed.

12 : As Amos, a pastoralist himself (7.14) knew, a was absolved of blame for loss of livestock if there were even small remains, , of a predator's attack. With sarcasm, Amos suggests that after the divine punishment, announced by the roar of a lion (1.2; 3.8), only scraps would remain from the palaces of Samaria. Thus, Israel's remains do not signify the survival of a righteous remnant, but rather they justify God's judgment, just as the remains of an animal's carcass vindicate the shepherd (Ex 22.13).
Hear, and testify against the house of Jacob, says the Lord God, the God of hosts:
13 : , the Northern Kingdom of Israel; cf. 9.8.
On the day I punish Israel for its transgressions, I will punish the altars of Bethel, and the horns of the altar shall be cut off and fall to the ground.

14 : was near the southern border of the Northern Kingdom, ca. 18 km (11 mi) north of Jerusalem. For Bethel as a shrine of the Northern Kingdom, see 1 Kings 12.25–30; for Amos and , see 4.4; 5.5–6; 7.10–13. Grasping , the projections at the corners of an altar, brought asylum (Ex 21.14; 1 Kings 2.28–34).
I will tear down the winter house as well as the summer house; and the houses of ivory shall perish, and the great houses shall come to an end, says the Lord.
3.1–6.14: Judgment speeches against Israel come in three sections, each with the same opening (3.1; 4.1; 5.1).
3.9–15: Neighboring nations are invited to witness the Lord's complaint against the elite of Israelite society, who dwelt in , massive fortified palaces.

15 : The will raze the tokens of conspicuous Israelite consumption: , and the decorated with carved inlay (see 6.4), such as the royal palace in Samaria built by Ahab (1 Kings 22.39) and whose is mentioned in 1 Kings 21.1.
